citations: Pencavel (1986) - labor supply response to wage changes

runs.reasoningSummary: Income elasticity of labor supply is typically small and positive for the overall adult population in the US. Literature suggests elasticities are concentrated in the range of 0.05–0.25, with considerable heterogeneity by demographics. A point estimate of 0.15 reflects a modest average response. The distribution is right-skewed to accommodate uncertainty and heterogeneous responses across population subgroups, with a modest left tail reflecting possible negative income effects for some groups.

citations: Saez, Slemrod & Giertz (2012) on income elasticity of taxable income and labor supply responses
Blundell & MaCurdy (1999) on labor supply elasticity estimation
Keane & Rogerson (2012) on macroeconomic approaches to labor supply
runs.reasoningSummary: Labor supply income elasticity in tax-benefit microsimulations typically reflects mixed income and substitution effects. For a broad U.S. adult population, the central literature suggests income elasticities are modest and often inelastic (near zero to moderately positive). Uncompensated elasticities tend to be low because income and substitution effects partially offset. I place the median around 0.15, with substantial uncertainty (p05 near zero or slightly negative, p95 around 0.40) to reflect heterogeneity across demographics and model specifications commonly used in policy analysis.

runs.interpretation: Global labor-supply income-response elasticity used in a U.S. microsimulation model, representing the percentage change in labor supply resulting from a 1 percent change in disposable income for adults in the United States.
citations: Kimmel & Kniesner (2013) labor supply meta-analysis
Chetty et al. (2012) sufficient statistics approach to income elasticities
Saez & Layard (2011) handbook chapter on optimal taxation
runs.reasoningSummary: Income elasticity of labor supply is typically estimated as modest and negative for aggregate populations in developed countries. Empirical labor economics finds uncompensated income effects generally range from -0.1 to -0.3, with substantial heterogeneity. U.S. microsimulation models often employ conservative mid-range estimates around -0.15. The negative sign reflects the normal good property—higher income typically reduces hours worked. For a bounded relative change in disposable income in a tax-benefit context, I estimate a central tendency near -0.15 with plausible range from -0.35 to -0.02.

Empirical labor economics finds uncompensated income effects generally range from -0.1 to -0.3, with substantial heterogeneity. U.S. microsimulation models often employ conservative mid-range estimates around -0.15. The negative sign reflects the normal good property\u2014higher income typically reduces hours worked. runs.reasoningSummary: Income elasticity of labor supply is typically small and often dominated by substitution effects in developed countries. runs.reasoningSummary: Income elasticity of labor supply is typically small and often negative or near-zero when accounting for standard life-cycle models and empirical estimates. My distribution centers near zero with rightward skew to accommodate the possibility of modest positive responses and acknowledges the empirical difficulty in isolating income effects from confounds.

runs.reasoningSummary: Income elasticity of labor supply tends toward zero or slightly positive for aggregate populations when estimated via income-compensated methods, but becomes modestly positive when including broader behavioral responses.
